speaker: interviewer
text: So you were liberated. And you were already in a hospital. They cured you, and you went to Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: You returned to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school, to [NON-ENGLISH]. I learned Hebrew. I learned-- I became very Zionistic minded now. I became-- I was-- I loved-- I loved everything about that. I saw [INAUDIBLE], I saw-- I saw all these people that I'd love to go to Israel. Unfortunately my father-- he shall rest in peace now-- he didn't want to go. He says Manny, I don't want to go. Because things were going very bad in Israel, with people getting killed, you know, from the survivors. No, he said, I want to have my kids with me. I don't want to look for nobody no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went where?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I met this lady. I met, now, an American lady that worked in [INAUDIBLE]. She was about, I don't know, 56 years old, that liked me very much. You know, she thought I was a very nice person, [? I ?] [? had ?] [? known her. ?] And she put my name up on the-- on the top to go to America. We went to America. We went-- we came to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: All three of you went together?

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- all three of us went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never did. Never did. I don't go back to places where I'm not liked.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to America in 1946, now, June the 24th, 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have the profession. So I looked for a job. And first thing, about a month, I was supported by, you know, the Jewish Appeal. They gave me-- they gave me clothes and they gave me food and everything. And then they-- they gave me a hotel to live on 100-- I lived on 103rd Street and Broadway, in a place called Marcel, Hotel Marcel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And from there, I went on 38th street, 37th Street took the subway, went to 38th Street. And I looked around. In the big building I saw names, I saw in the-- in the lobby. And I closed my eyes. And I picked one name. I picked, you know, I went up there. And I says, I'm Jewish. You know, please help me. Give me a job. It was lucky the man who had the factory was a-- was a man from Radom. And he gave me a job, he gave me. Then my brother start working there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of factory was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a garment factory. First thing I was a-- like, a examiner, picking up pins, you know. Then he told me that he'd learn me-- teach me how to sew.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was learning how to sew. I was learning, you know? And then he taught me how to put on fur, on-- on-- on-- on coats, you know. I did this, I did, now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he taught me how to make different, I know, things now. Then I-- I worked there for a-- '46, '47, '48-- til about '48, '49. Yeah, '49, worked there. Very nice people, you know. They didn't pay me too much money. But I was very happy to live in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father got a job right away, my father, working on 14th Street. He worked in a-- in a lady's store where they sell lady's wear. He was doing the alterations. I, my brother went with me. He worked with me. In 1949, my brother decided to go to-- to Los Angeles. Some of his-- one of his friends that went here by car, he said, I'm going along, too. I'm not staying in New York. It's too dangerous here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some-- some-- a taxi-- however you call it-- hit him in New York, and he-- and busted his nose, you know. So he went to Los Angeles, you know? And then after a while, I decided to come out here too, now.

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was here, I got a job, you know. I was a foreman in a place in Los Angeles, called [INAUDIBLE] California. And then I-- then I worked different place. I worked you know, mostly like, you know, the garment industry, I worked, you know. I was just-- my language was very limited. So I couldn't get the right type of job, you know. [? Anyway, ?] I got paid, you know. And I made my living. And I was very satisfied. In 1950, the war broked out in Korea. And I-- they-- went for my induction. Every time, it was-- I was always 1A. Every time, when I went to-- to the induction-- down on-- to the induction center, they used to say, well, go home. We're going to release you this time until further notice now. But this time, my brother was waiting outside for me with his-- with a friend in the car. And I went in to get the-- we had checked out. And this time they got me. And I-- and I was inducted in the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a citizen already?

speaker: interviewee
text: I became a citizen when I came to the Army. You know, when you swore-- when you swear in, in the Army, that-- that you have become an American soldier, you become-- you become a citizen when you lift up your hand and swear.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I took my basic training in Fort Ord, you know? And from there I became-- I went to East now. I was very happy. I was very happy I was in the Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I don't know, it felt very, very gratifying, some kind of feeling that I do something constructive instead of what I did before was nothing. Then after a while, I went to a small city. No, near when-- someplace in Kentucky, someplace. You know, from there they send me down to-- to Virginia, for four weeks to learn how to fix sewing machines. Because the Army needs fixing sewing machines. No, they were fixing typewriters, sewing machines, but becoming a mechanic, you know. And there I met my wife in the temple, on Friday night. She came with a friend of hers, she came. And then we were writing letters. We were writing. You know, when I was in-- then we shipped off to Korea. We shipped to Korea. You know, and from Korea, when we came back, you know, I married her.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to Korea--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you just practically just came out of another war. How did you feel when you saw those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you can't imagine how I felt, no. Here, I was on the other side of the fence, you know. I didn't know what to-- how to help them. I says, my golly, a few years ago I was in same position, the same predicament. How can I help them? I went to my-- man in charge. I said, look here. After dinner-- after chow, we call it in the Army-- you threw everything out. you know. You pour out. Give it to me. Let me give these people [? that. ?] it's like throwing-- you know, these people can live. The streets were not-- were so dusty. The people were so hungry, you know. And I-- and he said, OK, go ahead and do it. It was very gratifying. In fact-- in fact, they wrote about me, a story in the-- in the Jew-- in the-- in the soldier's paper throughout the country. It was called the-- I don't know what kind of paper they had. And they-- and I went and I helped these people out. And then afterwards, there was-- there was a leprosy colony right next to me. And I took the-- and I took the food in different kind of pieces of wood and coal and everything that we use, you know. Any leftover, I went to a rice [? paddle ?] with-- with food, til my knee and gave it them. They came down from the mountains, the people who had leprosy, and I gave it to them. And this was the only gratifying thing I have in Korea. I just-- I was able to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you get back from Korea?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came back in 1950-- November, 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: And got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- I worked for [? Adrian, ?] a designer in Los Angeles. And my brother took me in there. He was working there before. And from there, I-- I went to Virginia, married my wife, you know. And we were-- we've been married-- we-- we lived in California, we lived for a while. And then we went to Virginia, opened up a store, a children's shop, selling children's clothes. Unfortunately, I didn't like to live in the small city. That's where my wife was born, there, lived there. I came back here. From here, I lived-- I worked door-to-door, selling vacuum cleaners. And after, I opened up a-- no, I bought a gift shop on Hollywood Boulevard. I stayed there for a while. And from there, I couldn't get myself going. So I opened up [? in ?] [INAUDIBLE], a vacuum place, and sewing machines and typewriters. And that's what I was-- until 1987, I was doing that, now.

speaker: interviewer
text: You retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: I retired, now.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have three children, two girls and a boy. My oldest daughter is 41. My youngest daughter-- my old-- my-- my youngest-- my middle son, he'll be 40.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anita is the oldest one. And the-- the boy is 40, Gary. The youngest one is-- is 32.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Julie. I'd like to make a statement, something. When I came to America, and I married my wife and married the children, you know-- I-- I mean, I lived with the children and everything. You know? I must say that I was-- probably was a neurotic person. I went through a lot of things in my life, you know? And there was a lot of arguments. These adjustments, you know, to my wife, to-- to my kids, you know, they couldn't understand me. I couldn't understand them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was always in a way of persecution, a way of being afraid, being-- always, I had certain kind of ways. Somebody's going to catch me. Somebody's going to hurt me. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm sure I let it out on my kids, you know. And we had arguments about that, you know. So I'd like to apologize to my kids and my wife--

speaker: interviewer
text: You have grandchildren? --for being-- I have grandchildren-- grandchildren, two. I have two grandchildren, Paul and Janet. How old are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paul is 18, and Janet is 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, if you don't mind, we will stop now. And we will show some of your pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we will bring your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and introduce them.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you much.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I hope I was very good for the-- for the show.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1932, when my grandmother, my mother's mother, passed away. We put-- it's a [? matzevah, ?] which is the-- the stone on the cemetery. From the right side is my grandfather. Then my--

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Morhart. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grosfeld. Then my uncle, his-- my mother's brother, Yitzhrik Grosfeld. Then my aunt, and that's Blima Grosfeld. My father, Chaim Sztajnberg. My mother below, Milka Sztajnberg. And my-- and my mother's youngest brother, Abrum Steinberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Chaim Sztajnberg, with my mother, Milka Sztajnberg, When they got married, in 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Radom, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Milka Sztajnberg. Me in the right side now. And my left eye is my younger brother, [? Shlamic ?] Steinberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you find these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: This particular picture, I found in America, through an uncle, this one. Because my mother send it to her brother while he lived in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- well, this is my-- this is 1938. That's my father, Chaim Sztajnberg. In the right side is [? Shlamic ?] Steinberg. Me, in the left side, Mendel Steinberg, you know? And my youngest brother, Abraham Jakob Sztajnberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture-- OK, my name is Mendel Steinberg, you know? This is my first year in a cheder in Radom, Poland. And-- and I'm right next to the teacher, in the left side, the first guy next to the teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the top is my brother, [? Shlamic ?] Steinberg, you know? And this picture was found in Israel after the war. Thank you. Where was-- where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Radom, in our first year of cheder. In kinder--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken two weeks, when we arrived to America from Germany. From the right side is me, Mendel Steinberg. In the middle is my dad, Chaim Sztajnberg. And from the left side is my brother, [? Shlamic ?] Steinberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in New York, in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken in 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Radom, Poland, a year after my mother passed away. And we were-- erected this stone in the cemetery. To the right side is Chaim Sztajnberg, my father. Next to him is me, Mendel Steinberg. And in the left side is my brother, [? Shlamic ?] Steinberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, when we arrived to this country in 1953, walked on the street in Boston. And he passed out, passed away, had a heart attack and died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1945. When I came out from the hospital, a new uniform was given to me by the hospital. And this was about three months after I was liberated. This is Mendel Steinberg, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- this was taken in Mosbach, Germany. And if you notice, I gained a little bit of weight here, and I looked much better. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken while I was in the Army and furloughed in Los Angeles. And I came and did my basic training and I-- before I went to Korea, my brother and I, we said goodbye, you know? We took a picture before I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Mendel Steinberg, you know. My brother's name is [? Shlamic ?] Steinberg, you know. Taken approximate in 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture, [? Mimi ?] and I just got married. Mendel Steinberg, from the right side. [? Mimi, ?] on the left site, Mimi Steinberg. We got married on February the 15th, 1953. This picture was-- was taken in New York, 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this picture was taken on about my-- my granddaughter's bat mitzvah. And the right site is Gary Steinberg, my son. Next to him is my-- myself, Mendel Steinberg. You know? Next is my son-in-law, [? Ron ?] Lagrassa. Next to him is my daughter, Anita Lagrassa.

speaker: interviewee
text: And next to him is Mimi, my wife, Mimi Steinberg. Then my grandson, Paul Lagrassa. And the bottom is my granddaughter, Janet Lagrassa. It was taken at-- at-- at Janet's bat mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken at the Ventura House in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken 1993-- excuse me-- At Janet's bat mitzvah, 1990-- Janet's bat mitzvah. And the right side is my son-in-law, Frank Orsatti. Then it's Janet. Then it's his wife, Julie Orsatti.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was taken at Janet's bat mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Ventura Club in Los Angeles-- in San Fernando Valley.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Steinberg, will you introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd be delighted. In the-- in the left-- In the left side is my son-in-law, Ron Lagrassa. RON (SON-IN-LAW): Hi. Next to him is my daughter, Anita Lagrassa. ANITA (DAUGHTER): Hello. Next to him-- next to her is my son, Gary Steinberg. Next to him is my son-in-law, Frank Orsatti. And then his wife, Julie Orsatti. [LAUGHS] JULIE (DAUGHTER): I'm your daughter. Then my grandson, Paul Lagrassa. And my granddaughter, Janet Lagrassa. And my lovely wife, Mimi Steinberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mimi Steinberg. I would like to know, how did you manage to marry a Holocaust survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: MIMI (WIFE): Well, it's really quite a story. I was just 16 when I met him. He happened to be sent to Virginia, to-- with the Army. They sent him for some kind of studies for about six weeks. And I was in high school. And one evening, a cousin of mine asked me to go out to the Army camp for dances. And they had services for the young Jewish fellows on Tuesday night. And then they'd go to the service clubs afterwards. And we went over there that evening. And I met him on the steps of the 'synagogue' that was there. And then we went dancing afterwards. And I guess I think we had just one date and he was shipped off to Korea after that. And we corresponded for two years. And while I was in college, he wrote me and said, I want to marry you. And it was very fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what a Holocaust survivor was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I really didn't. Actually, I'm from a very small town in Virginia, 17,000 people. And very few Jewish people. I think maybe 13 families in all. And we really, until we-- until I met Manny, we hadn't heard anything about the concentration camps or the survivors or anything. It was totally new to me at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect your life, being married to a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's very difficult. I don't know that it was the fact that he was a survivor, or the fact that our backgrounds were very different. I was brought up in a very small town and rather naive, I must say. And he was European and different. It was a very difficult few years, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long have you-- MIMI (WIFE): But he was-- always been a very good provider and a very loving person. How long have you been married? MIMI (WIFE): It'll be 43 years this month, as a matter of fact. Well, you managed pretty well. MIMI (WIFE): I think so. Anita? ANITA (DAUGHTER): Mhm? We hear so much about second generation having problems with parents. And how was it for you, to be the first child born to survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: ANITA (DAUGHTER): It was very difficult. However, now that I'm older, I-- I can reflect back on those years, having my own children. And I can see that possibly, not only did my dad not have a teenage life, because he was never a teenager-- he was in the camps during those years-- but I think also, just being a dad, he just didn't understand a teenage daughter. And I think most men, for that part, find it very difficult to have their little girls grow up. So at the time, I didn't understand that. I just knew it was hard. And I wanted to get out as soon as I could. But I think, all in all, I grew up and I love him very much and he loves me. And we're happy now. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ron, did you know that you're marrying a second generation, child of a survivor? Did that mean anything to you? RON (SON-IN-LAW): No, it didn't mean anything to me at the time. How about now? RON (SON-IN-LAW): Well, I-- I didn't know then what was involved. I didn't get to know my-- my in-laws right away. And then as the years went on and our marriage continued, I was able to learn more about it and have more sensitivity to that time in his life. Gary, how was life for you growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: GARY (SON): At the time I-- when I was a child, I thought it was normal. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had-- being a-- a Holocaust survivor, my father, I never made excuses for him because of that. I didn't think it had anything to do with it. As-- when I got-- when I got older, I started looking back. And I realized that he was in the camps from 13 years old to 18 years old. And after I passed 18, I realized what that meant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: GARY (SON): And I realize how hard it was for him to relate to us when we were teenagers.

speaker: interviewer
text: ANITA (DAUGHTER): Mhm. He was never a teenager. ANITA (DAUGHTER): No. GARY (SON): Right. He lost all those years. ANITA (DAUGHTER): Mhm. I-- I was wondering whether any of you really thought about that while you were growing up. GARY (SON): I've thought about it a lot. A lot. Julie, how about you? JULIE (DAUGHTER): Well, being that I am eight years younger than my sister and six years younger than my brother, I basically grew up as the only child here. My parents, I never really thought about him being in the Holocaust. I just loved my parents. And as everybody should, or is supposed to. It seemed normal to me. By the time you were born, they were American. JULIE (DAUGHTER): Right. So I never really thought about him being anything else, other than American. Frank? Does that make a difference in your life, that she is a child--

speaker: interviewee
text: FRANK (SON-IN-LAW): No. I met Julie-- I didn't even know that much about her parents. But after finding out about what Manny had been through, I had a lot of admiration for anybody who can survive and-- and go through what he did. Because I've seen some movies and read  some books and heard some stories from-- see, I was a merchant seaman for a while. And guys had been around and had parents and known people that went through it. And it impressed, me, the fact that he could arrive in a city broke and-- and get married and provide for a family the way he has, and come to the Valley in California, and buy a home, and do what he did. I can't believe that the guy who'd go through all that really arrived-- arrived with a-- I think it was five cents and a picture of his mother and his shoe or something, and put his head to the grindstone and try and put that behind him, which nobody can ever do. But it gave me a lot of admiration for him and anybody else that experienced anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of you join the Second Generation, Children of Survivors? ANITA (DAUGHTER): We're-- Did you belong  to Second-- ANITA (DAUGHTER): We're-- we're all in it. We get the newsletter and everything else. Wow. Janet, what does that mean to you, that your grandfather is a Holocaust survivor? JANET (GRANDDAUGHTER): Um, I don't know-- I don't know. It just-- He tells me stories. And I like, I envy my grandpa for everything he's done. And I've learned about it from school. And in my classes we talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it shocks me, like, what  he went through. And I love my grandpa very much. And-- and then I can, you know, it's just-- I don't know. I just look at it as my grandpa was in that and I-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Paul, how about you? PAUL (GRANDSON): Me? Yes. PAUL (GRANDSON): Well--

speaker: interviewee
text: JANET (GRANDDAUGHTER): Cat got my tongue. PAUL (GRANDSON): I don't know. He's-- he's always been there for me. I'm kind of-- I'm not the smartest guy in the world. Or I've always had problems in school and stuff. And he's always been there for me, helping me out, you know, always just-- just trying to be there for me. [LAUGHS] And I like-- I like to be with him. It's-- it's fun. I come over here and you know, we go out to dinner. And we spend the days together and stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does-- what does Judaism mean to the two of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: PAUL (GRANDSON): Well, I grew up in a family with-- you know, my mom's Jewish and my dad's of Catholic religion. And we were never brought up to go to church or temple. But-- JANET (GRANDDAUGHTER): I had a bat mitzvah. Yeah. My sis had a bat mitzvah. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: I saw the pictures. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: PAUL (GRANDSON): I don't know, sometimes I regret that I didn't have a bar mitzvah. But other than that, I-- you know, my mom's Jewish. So I guess, you know, I'm a-- also Jewish person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Manny, a final message to your family, to humanity. What is it you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, number one, I'd like to apologize, like I said to you before, to my wife, to my kids now. I didn't treat them right when they was young. I couldn't understand them. It was like, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just-- I just couldn't-- I just couldn't acclimate myself to the right time, and to-- to them. Because I was laid in the bed and I thought about what happened to me, you know? And I didn't get sympathy or something. I didn't get it, you know? So I used to pick on certain issues, you know. But while time went on, I understood, you know? After all, I got to keep the machine going, the love going. So I still love them [? now. ?] And I love my whole family, you know? And every time I get up in the morning, I thank God I got them. Do you ever take them to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took my grandson to Israel. I took my son-in-law to Israel. He-- he went there twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you can be very proud of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I thank you, all of you, for joining us. ANITA (DAUGHTER): You're welcome. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. RON (SON-IN-LAW): You're welcome. GARY (SON): Thank you. Thank you.
